summary:
Glucagon mainly works through a mechanism involving its interaction with external cellular surfaces before triggering an internal response; it does not directly cross into the cytoplasmic space as per typical hormonal action patterns described for these substances. Among those mentioned, thyroid hormone uniquely enters the cell where it can interact within the nucleus without needing to activate any intermediary pathways at the plasma membrane level. \boxed{T}
The summary focuses on highlighting how different endocrine signals operate versus which one specifically involves entering the cell interior post-receptor signaling initiation. This aligns closely with identifying unique properties among similar classes of molecules based solely off their mode-of-action profiles given here about specific peptide/hormone interactions across membranes during physiological regulation processes. 

Key Points:

- Most discussed hormones don't passively diffuse past lipid bilayers
- Specific mention made regarding glucose-regulating factors 
- Distinction drawn between active vs passive entry routes taken up by distinct types of regulatory compounds
To refine further while staying concise yet comprehensive...

Glucagon operates externally using GPCRs whereas most others use secondary messengers/transporters. Only T4/T3 actually penetrate fully into nuclei making them stand out mechanistically from peers.\boxed{T}

This version emphasizes core distinctions more clearly & concisely addressing both asked aspects simultaneously efficiently.
